consider this reference format: Jonides, J., Schumacher, E. H., Smith, E. E., Koeppe, R. A., Awh, E., Reuter-Lorenz, P. A., Marshuetz, C., & Willis, C. R. (1998). The role of parietal cortex in verbal working memory. Journal of Neuroscience, 18(13), 5026–5034.

Jonides, J., Schumacher, E. H., Smith, E. E., Lauber, E. J., Awh, E., Minoshima, S., & Koeppe, R. A. (1997). Verbal working memory load affects regional brain activation as measured by PET. Journal of Cognitive Neuroscience, 9(4), 462–475.

Identify key formatting elements

content.text:
These references follow **APA (American Psychological Association) format**. Key characteristics:

1. **Author format**: Last name, First initial(s), separated by commas, with "&" before the last author
2. **Year**: In parentheses after authors
3. **Title**: Only first word and proper nouns capitalized
4. **Journal**: Title case, italicized
5. **Volume(issue)**, page numbers

message.content:
here: Elsevier
Clinical Neurophysiology
Volume 128, Issue 6, June 2017, Pages 882-891
Clinical Neurophysiology
Spatio-temporal dynamics of working memory maintenance and scanning of verbal information
Author links open overlay panel
Toshimune Kambara a d 1
, 
Erik C. Brown e 1
, 
Jeong-Won Jeong a b
, 
Noa Ofen c f
, 
Yasuo Nakai a
, 
Eishi Asano a b

Show more

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.clinph.2017.03.005
Get rights and content
Highlights
•
Intracranial electrocorticography recording revealed high-gamma augmentation during working memory maintenance in the left precentral gyrus.
•
Such precentral high-gamma augmentation was larger when memory load was increased.
•
Left inferior-frontal and supra-marginal gyri showed high-gamma augmentation during scanning.

content.text:

PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6436923/"},{"title":"Persistent hippocampal neural firing and hippocampal-cortical coupling predict verbal working memory load | Science Advances","url":"https://advances.sciencemag.org/content/5/3/eaav3687.abstract"},{"title":"Cognitive neuroscience perspective on memory: overview and summary - Based on the search results, I found comprehensive information about Boran et al.'s 2019 study published in Science Advances. Here are the key findings:

## Study Overview

The study was published on March 27, 2019 in Science Advances (volume 5, issue 3, article eaav3687) by Ece Boran, Tommaso Fedele, Peter Klaver, Peter Hilfiker, Lennart Stieglitz, Thomas Grunwald, and Johannes Sarnthein from institutions including UniversitätsSpital Zürich and ETH Zürich.

## Main Findings

### 1. Persistent Hippocampal Neural Firing

The researchers recorded single neurons and intracranial EEG in the medial temporal lobe while human subjects maintained sets of letters in working memory. They found that:

- Hippocampal neural firing differentiated between success and error trials during stimulus encoding, predicted workload during memory maintenance, and predicted the subjects' behavior during retrieval
- The hippocampus contained a significant percentage of neurons that increased their firing rate under high workloads
- More maintenance neurons were found in the hippocampus for trials with correct compared to incorrect performance

### 2. Hippocampal-Cortical Coupling

During maintenance, neuronal firing was synchronized with intracranial hippocampal EEG. On the network level, synchronization between hippocampal and scalp EEG in the theta-alpha frequency range showed workload dependent oscillatory coupling between hippocampus and cortex.

Key aspects of this coupling:
- Workload-dependent coupling occurred in eight of nine subjects in individual frequency bands (range: 5.5 to 16.5 Hz; median band limits: 7.8 to 10 Hz)
- The phase-locking value (PLV) during maintenance exceeded the PLV during encoding in eight of nine subjects

### 3. Verbal Working Memory Load Effects

The study demonstrated that:
- Maintenance neurons increased and decreased firing in the hippocampus and increased firing in the entorhinal cortex for high compared to low workload
- Population firing predicted workload particularly during the maintenance period
- Prediction accuracy of workload based on single-trial activity during maintenance was strongest for neurons in the entorhinal cortex and hippocampus

### 4. Posterior-Anterior Gradient

The study found a gradual increase in the PLV toward the posterior end of the hippocampal axis (34 electrode pairs; R² = 0.118, P = 0.0467), suggesting regional specialization along the hippocampal axis.

## Conclusions

The researchers concluded that persistent neural activity in the hippocampus participated in working memory processing that is specific to memory maintenance, load sensitive and synchronized to the cortex. The data suggest that persistent neural firing in the MTL reflects a domain-general process of maintenance supporting performance and workload of multiple items in working memory below and beyond working memory capacity.

This study provides important evidence for the role of the hippocampus in working memory maintenance, traditionally associated more with long-term memory formation, and demonstrates how hippocampal-cortical coupling supports cognitive load during verbal working memory tasks.

content.text:
## Key Findings from Boran et al. (2019):

**1. Persistent Hippocampal Firing**
- Hippocampal neurons showed sustained firing that predicted working memory performance
- Firing rates increased with higher memory loads (more letters to remember)
- Neural activity differentiated between successful and error trials

**2. Hippocampal-Cortical Coupling**
- Synchronization between hippocampus and cortex in theta-alpha frequencies (7.8-10 Hz median)
- This coupling was stronger during memory maintenance than encoding
- Coupling strength predicted memory load across subjects

**3. Gradient Along Hippocampus**
- Stronger cortical coupling toward the posterior hippocampus
- Suggests regional specialization along the hippocampal axis

**4. Working Memory Support**
- Hippocampus actively maintains information during working memory delays
- Challenges traditional view of hippocampus as primarily for long-term memory
- Persistent firing reflects domain-general maintenance process supporting multiple items

This study used intracranial recordings from epilepsy patients to demonstrate that the hippocampus plays an active role in maintaining verbal information in working memory through sustained neural firing and cortical synchronization.

message.content: is this accurtate: "Similarly, Boran et al. (2019) showed that hippocampal neurons exhibit persistent activity during maintenance of letters, with firing rates scaling with memory load and coupling with frontoparietal areas in the theta-alpha range"

content.text:
Yes, this statement is accurate with one minor clarification:

✓ **Hippocampal neurons exhibit persistent activity during maintenance of letters** - Correct
✓ **Firing rates scaling with memory load** - Correct
✓ **Coupling in the theta-alpha range** - Correct (7.8-10 Hz median)

**Minor clarification**: The paper doesn't specifically mention "frontoparietal areas." Instead, it refers to:
- "Hippocampal-cortical coupling" (general cortical areas)
- Scalp EEG recordings showing synchronization
- A posterior-anterior gradient along the hippocampus

The coupling was measured between hippocampal intracranial EEG and scalp EEG, which would include frontoparietal regions, but the paper doesn't explicitly identify these specific cortical areas. Your statement is essentially correct and captures the main findings accurately.

content.text:
## Basis of My Understanding:

From the search results, I found:
1. **PubMed abstract** and **PMC full text** links
2. **Science Advances journal page** with the article
3. **NASA/ADS abstract** 
4. The search revealed key details about persistent firing, workload-dependent activity, and theta-alpha coupling

## More Accurate Rephrasing:

"Similarly, Boran et al. (2019) showed that hippocampal neurons exhibit persistent activity during maintenance of letters, with firing rates scaling with memory load and showing synchronization between hippocampus and cortical areas via scalp EEG in the theta-alpha range (7.8-10 Hz)."

## Key Clarification:

The original statement said "coupling with frontoparietal areas" but the paper actually measured coupling between:
- **Intracranial hippocampal EEG** (recorded directly from the hippocampus)
- **Scalp EEG** (which captures activity from various cortical regions)

The paper didn't specifically identify which cortical regions were involved in the coupling, just that hippocampal-cortical synchronization occurred. The scalp EEG would include frontoparietal areas, but this wasn't explicitly stated in the findings I found.

newTodos.content: Help user fix MS Word indentation/tab issues with references

content.text:
Here are several solutions for stubborn indentation in MS Word:

## Quick Fixes:

1. **Clear All Formatting**
   - Select the problematic text
   - Press `Ctrl+Spacebar` (Windows) or `Cmd+Spacebar` (Mac)
   - Or click the "Clear All Formatting" button (eraser icon) in Home tab

2. **Show/Hide Formatting Marks**
   - Click ¶ button in Home tab to see hidden formatting
   - Look for arrows (→) indicating tabs or other marks
   - Delete visible tab marks directly

3. **Use Ruler**
   - View → Ruler (if not visible)
   - Drag the indent markers (triangles) on ruler all the way left
   - Select text and drag both top and bottom triangles together

4. **Paragraph Dialog Box**
   - Right-click → Paragraph
   - Set all indents to 0
   - Under "Special," select "None"

5. **Nuclear Option**
   - Copy text to Notepad (strips all formatting)
   - Copy from Notepad back to Word
   - Reformat as needed

## Most Common Cause:
References often get "hanging indent" formatting that's hard to remove. Check if it's a style issue by changing the paragraph style to "Normal."

newTodos.content: Explain how to convert all caps text to title case in MS Word

content.text:
## Quick Method:

1. **Select the ALL CAPS text**
2. **Press Shift+F3** repeatedly to cycle through:
   - all lowercase
   - Title Case
   - ALL CAPS

## Alternative Method:

1. **Select the text**
2. **Home tab → Change Case button (Aa icon)**
3. **Choose "Capitalize Each Word"** for title case

## Keyboard Shortcut:
- **Shift+F3** cycles through case options
- Keep pressing until you get the format you want

newTodos.content: Provide Mac-specific instructions for changing case in MS Word

content.text:
## For Mac:

1. **Select the ALL CAPS text**
2. **Format menu → Change Case**
3. **Choose "Title Case"**

## Or use the Ribbon:

1. **Select the text**
2. **Home tab → Change Case button (Aa icon)**
3. **Choose "Capitalize Each Word"**

## Mac Keyboard Shortcut:
- **Fn+Shift+F3** (you need to add the Fn key on Mac)
- Some Macs require going to System Preferences → Keyboard → Use F1, F2, etc. keys as standard function keys

The Shift+F3 doesn't work on Mac without the Fn key because F3 is assigned to Mission Control by default.
